title: The Great Famine and the End of the Boom
subtitle: the climate cools, the harvest fails, and Europe runs out of food
bullets: Around 1300 the Medieval Warm Period ended and the Little Ice Age began, cooling European summers by roughly 1°C, shortening growing seasons, and pushing marginal farmland out of viability across the continent.

The Great Famine of 1315–1317 — three consecutive summers of catastrophic rain — killed an estimated 10% of northern Europe's population in the largest demographic shock between the Plague of Justinian and the Black Death.
The Great Bovine Pestilence of 1319–1320, probably rinderpest, killed perhaps 60% of European cattle and oxen, compounding the famine by destroying the draft animals that pulled the plows.

Europe in 1300 had perhaps 75–85 million people, with Paris (200,000) the largest city; the population was 90% rural, the Catholic Church owned roughly a third of cultivated land, and Latin connected literate society from Iceland to Sicily.

Dante Alighieri finished the Divine Comedy around 1320, an epic in vernacular Tuscan rather than Latin whose spherical-earth cosmology demonstrates that educated medieval Europeans never believed in a flat earth — that myth was invented in the nineteenth century.

Petrarch climbed Mont Ventoux in 1336 and rediscovered Cicero's lost letters in 1345, inventing the cult of the individual genius and the systematic recovery of classical Latin manuscripts that historians later called Renaissance humanism.

King Philip IV of France arrested every Templar in his kingdom on Friday October 13, 1307, extracted heresy confessions under torture, and pressured Pope Clement V to suppress the order — the moment royal bureaucracies first demonstrated they could destroy a universal religious institution.

Climate stress around 1300 was a Eurasian phenomenon: the same Little Ice Age cooling stressed Mongol pastoralism on the steppe, contributing to the political instability that would eventually break the Mongol Empire and the Silk Road dislocations that would carry the plague west.

title: The Black Death
subtitle: Yersinia pestis arrives via Genoese galleys and kills a third of Europe in four years
bullets: Twelve Genoese trading galleys from the besieged Crimean port of Caffa put in at Messina in October 1347 with crews dying of buboes — the conventional starting point of the Black Death's European phase, brought by ships' rats and oriental rat fleas.

By 1351 the disease had reached every corner of Europe and killed roughly a third of the continent's population — perhaps 30–50% — making it the worst demographic catastrophe in recorded European history.

Modern paleogenomics has definitively identified the disease as Yersinia pestis, the bacterium of bubonic plague, ending a long historical debate by recovering plague DNA from mass graves at sites including East Smithfield in London.

Contemporaries did not call it the Black Death — they called it the Great Mortality or the Pestilence; the phrase Black Death came into popular use in nineteenth-century Scandinavian and German histories.

The Mongol khan Jani Beg reportedly catapulted plague-infected corpses over the walls of the Genoese trading post at Caffa in 1346, one of the first attested instances of biological warfare and the conventional vector for the disease's escape into the Mediterranean.

The post-plague labor shortage roughly doubled European wages by 1400 and broke the manorial labor system in western Europe, producing a paradox in which the catastrophe that killed a third of the population materially improved life for the survivors.

Strasbourg burned 900 Jews alive on Saint Valentine's Day, February 14, 1349, before the plague had even reached the city; pogroms across the Rhineland and Catalonia destroyed most western European Jewish communities and pushed the Ashkenazi demographic center east into Poland.

The Andalusi physician Ibn al-Khatib argued in 1349 for direct human-to-human contagion against the prevailing miasma theory — one of the most modern-sounding pieces of medieval medical thinking on either side of the Mediterranean.

Boccaccio's Decameron, finished around 1353 and framed by ten young Florentines fleeing the plague to a country villa, became the first major prose masterpiece in Italian and the founding text of the late-medieval cult of death.

title: The Hundred Years War
subtitle: longbows destroy chivalry, Joan of Arc reverses the war, French artillery ends it
bullets: Edward III of England claimed the French throne through his mother Isabella in 1337 — a thin dynastic pretext for what was really a war over English continental possessions, French royal authority over Gascony, and control of the Flemish wool trade.

At Crécy on August 26, 1346, English longbowmen and dismounted men-at-arms destroyed a French chivalric host three times their size, with perhaps 4,000 to 10,000 French dead including 1,500 nobles and 200 English casualties — the death of high-medieval warfare in a single afternoon.

At Poitiers in 1356, the Black Prince captured King John II of France alive; the Treaty of Brétigny in 1360 ceded most of southwestern France to England in exchange for John's release, but French recovery under Charles V the Wise had reversed it within a generation.

Henry V's English army of perhaps 6,000 destroyed a French force three times larger at Agincourt on October 25, 1415, and Henry famously ordered captured French nobles killed in cold blood mid-battle — an episode Shakespeare omitted from his play.

Joan of Arc, a sixteen-year-old peasant girl from Domrémy who claimed to hear the voices of Saints Catherine and Margaret and the Archangel Michael, broke the English siege of Orléans on May 8, 1429, and led the Dauphin Charles to his coronation as Charles VII at Reims Cathedral.

Joan was captured by Burgundian forces at Compiègne in 1430, sold to the English, tried at Rouen by pro-English clerics, and burned alive on May 30, 1431 at age nineteen; her conviction was overturned in 1456 and she was canonized in 1920.

Charles VII's reformed army, equipped with bronze cannons cast under the Bureau brothers, caught John Talbot's English force at Castillon on July 17, 1453 — French artillery destroyed disciplined infantry the way English longbows had destroyed cavalry a century earlier.

The Hundred Years War left France with a strong centralized crown, a permanent direct tax called the taille, and a standing army; it left England with a bankrupted exchequer that would shortly collapse into the Wars of the Roses.

title: The Avignon Papacy and the Western Schism
subtitle: the universal Church splits into rival popes and the moral authority of the papacy never recovers
bullets: King Philip IV's chief minister physically arrested Pope Boniface VIII at Anagni in September 1303 — the inverse of Henry IV's penance at Canossa in 1077 — demonstrating that a French king could now have a universal pope arrested without serious consequence.

Pope Clement V relocated the papal court to Avignon in 1309 and never left; the Avignon Papacy lasted 68 years and seven popes, all of them French, in what Petrarch called the Babylonian Captivity of the Church.

The Avignon popes built the Palais des Papes (still one of the largest Gothic buildings in the world), funded their court through annates and indulgences extracted from the rest of Christendom, and were widely seen as French royal puppets.

Pope Gregory XI returned to Rome in 1377 under pressure from the Dominican mystic Catherine of Siena; he died fourteen months later, and the disputed conclave that followed produced two rival popes, beginning the Western Schism.

From 1378 to 1417, two and at one point three rival popes simultaneously claimed the throne of Saint Peter — France and Castile recognized Avignon, England and the Empire recognized Rome, and lay populations were told their salvation depended on choosing the correct pope with no available means of determination.

The Council of Constance from 1414 to 1418 was the largest church council ever held — 600 bishops, thousands of others — and ended the Schism by deposing all three rival popes and electing Martin V in 1417.

Constance also issued the conciliarist decree Haec Sancta in 1415, declaring that a general council holds power immediately from Christ — the closest medieval Christianity ever came to constitutional reform of the papacy.

The papacy patiently undermined the conciliarist movement over the next half-century and won the long argument, leaving the Church without an institutional reform mechanism — a victory that would make the sixteenth-century Reformation effectively unavoidable.

title: Wycliffe, Hus, and the Late-Medieval Reformations
subtitle: the Reformation rehearsed in England and Bohemia a century before Luther
bullets: John Wycliffe at Oxford in the 1370s and 1380s articulated nearly every doctrine Luther would later adopt — scriptural supremacy, attack on indulgences, attack on clerical wealth, denial of papal authority, rejection of transubstantiation, demand for vernacular Bibles.

The Wycliffite Bible, translated by Wycliffe and his Lollard followers from the Latin Vulgate around 1382, was the first complete English Bible — about 250 manuscript copies survive despite suppression, suggesting thousands more once existed.

Wycliffe died of natural causes in 1384, but the Council of Constance ordered his bones exhumed, burned, and the ashes scattered in a stream in 1428 — posthumous burning being the Church's way of declaring his heresies too dangerous for consecrated ground.

Jan Hus, preaching in Czech at the Bethlehem Chapel in Prague, adopted Wycliffe's positions and was burned alive at the Council of Constance on July 6, 1415 — despite holding a written safe-conduct from Emperor Sigismund.

The defenestration of seven Catholic city councillors out the windows of the New Town Hall in Prague in 1419 began the Hussite Wars, which fought five papal-imperial Crusades to a standstill between 1419 and 1434.

Jan Žižka, the brilliant one-eyed (then blind) Czech commander, invented the war wagon — armored ox-drawn wagons fitted with handgonnes and small cannons — that would shortly be adopted by the Ottomans and other gunpowder armies.

The Compactata of Prague in 1436 gave Bohemia a semi-independent national church with reform doctrines and lay communion in both kinds, two centuries before the pattern became routine — the only time the late medieval Catholic Church formally permitted distinct reformed practice in any region.

Luther reportedly remarked at the Leipzig Debate in 1519, when accused of espousing Hussite heresies, that he was a Hussite without knowing it — a useful summary of the doctrinal continuity that the printing press would shortly carry from Bohemia to all of Germany.

title: Peasant Revolts and Urban Uprisings
subtitle: post-plague labor leverage produces three of the largest popular revolts of medieval Europe
bullets: The Jacquerie of May–June 1358 was the largest peasant revolt in French history before 1789, named for the noble nickname Jacques Bonhomme; perhaps 5,000 peasants under Guillaume Cale attacked noble manors across the Île-de-France in the chaos after Poitiers.

Charles II of Navarre lured Cale to a parley under safe conduct, decapitated him on the spot, then attacked the leaderless peasant army on June 10, 1358; the noble revenge across Beauvaisis killed thousands more in the following weeks.

The Ciompi Revolt in Florence in July 1378 was the only direct rule by industrial workers in any major medieval European city — Florentine wool-carders held the Signoria for six weeks before guild masters and patricians overthrew them on August 31.

The Ciompi episode is sometimes called the first proletarian revolution; it was defeated by exactly the structural disadvantages that working-class organization would face in capitalist economies for the next five centuries.

The English Peasants' Revolt of 1381 was triggered by the regressive 1380 Poll Tax; rebels under Wat Tyler and the radical priest John Ball marched on London, burned the Savoy Palace, and killed the Archbishop of Canterbury and the royal treasurer.

John Ball preached at Blackheath on June 12, 1381 from the rhyming text 'When Adam delved and Eve span, who was then the gentleman?' — a Lollard-tinged egalitarian theology rooted in Wycliffe and the post-plague labor empowerment.

The fourteen-year-old Richard II met the rebels at Smithfield on June 15; Mayor William Walworth killed Wat Tyler with a dagger, and Richard rode forward and reportedly cried 'I will be your captain!' to calm the leaderless crowd long enough for the city militia to arrive.

The three revolts achieved no immediate political goals but contributed to a quiet structural transformation in which English serfdom became unenforceable by 1500 — while Eastern European serfdom intensified into the early modern Second Serfdom for entirely different structural reasons.

The contemporary Red Turban Rebellion in China overthrew the Yuan dynasty in 1368 and produced the Ming founding under Zhu Yuanzhang — the largest peasant-revolt success in world history and the only late-medieval rural uprising to produce a long-term political revolution.

title: The Renaissance Threshold
subtitle: Italian city-states reinvent painting, architecture, banking, and printing collapses the cost of books
bullets: The Italian Renaissance was less a sudden break than the late-medieval Italian city-state world entering its mature phase — Burckhardt invented the term in 1860, and modern medievalists rightly insist the supposed Renaissance discoveries were extensions of late medieval developments.

Five competing Italian city-states — Venice, Genoa, Milan, Florence, and Naples plus the Papal States — produced the patronage rivalry that drove the cultural flowering, formalized by the Peace of Lodi in 1454 and lasting until the French invasion of 1494.

Cosimo de' Medici dominated Florentine politics for thirty years after 1434 without ever holding formal office, ran the largest bank in Europe, founded the Platonic Academy under Marsilio Ficino, and hosted the Council of Florence in 1438.

Filippo Brunelleschi's double-shelled brick dome on Florence Cathedral, finished in 1436 after sixteen years of construction without exterior scaffolding, was the largest masonry dome built in Europe since the Pantheon thirteen centuries earlier.

Donatello's bronze David around 1440 was the first freestanding nude statue cast in Europe since classical antiquity; Masaccio's Brancacci Chapel frescoes in 1424–1427 founded Renaissance painting before Masaccio died at twenty-six.

The van Eyck brothers' Ghent Altarpiece in 1432 and Jan van Eyck's Arnolfini Portrait in 1434 perfected oil painting and produced a Northern Renaissance independent of and parallel to the Italian one, with microscopic naturalistic detail that no fifteenth-century Italian artist could match.

Hieronymus Bosch painted his hallucinatory Garden of Earthly Delights triptych around 1490–1510 in 's-Hertogenbosch, populating his canvases with grotesque hybrid creatures and proto-surrealist imagery without parallel in late-medieval art.

Johannes Gutenberg's 42-line Bible appeared in Mainz around 1455; by 1500 presses had been set up in 200 European cities and printed roughly 20 million books — more than the entire previous millennium of European manuscript culture combined.

Lorenzo de' Medici ruled Florence from 1469 to 1492 and patronized Botticelli, the young Michelangelo, and Pico della Mirandola while letting the Medici Bank slide toward the bankruptcy that destroyed the family two years after his death.

title: The End of an Era
subtitle: Constantinople falls, Granada surrenders, Columbus sails, and the Middle Ages mutate into the early modern world
bullets: Sultan Mehmed II's Ottoman cannons under the Hungarian renegade Orban — including the 27-foot Basilica gun firing 1,200-pound stone balls — battered the Theodosian Walls of Constantinople from April 6 to May 29, 1453, ending the Roman Empire after 1,123 continuous years.

Mehmed's brilliant logistical coup of April 22, 1453 — dragging 70 ships overland on greased logs across the ridge behind Galata into the upper Golden Horn — opened a new front against the harbor walls and forced the defenders to spread thin.

Constantine XI Palaiologos, the last Roman emperor, was killed in the fighting near the Saint Romanus Gate; his body was never positively identified, producing the legend of the Marble Emperor that survived in Greek folk memory until the nineteenth century.

The Wars of the Roses (1455–1485) destroyed most of the medieval English aristocracy in dynastic civil war between Lancaster and York; Henry Tudor's victory over Richard III at Bosworth Field on August 22, 1485 began 118 years of Tudor rule and the early modern English state.
Richard III was the last English king to die in personal combat on a battlefield; his bones were rediscovered under a Leicester city-council car park in 2012 and reburied in Leicester Cathedral.

Granada surrendered to Ferdinand and Isabella on January 2, 1492, completing the 781-year Reconquista of Iberia from Muslim political rule; the Alhambra Decree three months later expelled all Jews from Castile and Aragon, scattering Sephardic refugees across the Mediterranean.

Christopher Columbus sailed from Palos on August 3, 1492 with three ships and 90 men, sighted land at the Bahamian island of Guanahani on October 12, and died in 1506 still believing he had reached the eastern fringes of Asia.

Bartolomeu Dias rounded the Cape of Good Hope in 1488 and Vasco da Gama reached Calicut on the Malabar Coast on May 20, 1498, completing the first sea voyage from Europe to India and beginning the Portuguese maritime empire that would dominate the Indian Ocean for a century.

The candidates for when the Middle Ages ended — 1453, 1485, 1492, 1500, 1517, 1648 — none of them mark a clean break; the period of transition runs from roughly 1450 to 1550 with no single year decisive, and the European who fell asleep in 1300 and woke in 1500 would have found very little of his world recognizable.

By 1500 every major continental population was within decades of being interconnected — the diseases, technologies, crops, and ideas that had been regional were about to become global, and the working-out of that interconnection is the next five centuries of human history.
